title: Cauda Equina Syndrome
oneLiner: Cauda Equina Syndrome (CES) is a surgical emergency caused by compression of lumbosacral nerve roots below the conus medullaris, threatening permanent paralysis and sphincter loss.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic Vignette:** A 45-year-old woman presents to ED with a 2-day history of worsening lower back pain after lifting a heavy box. She now reports numbness in the 'saddle area' (buttocks, perineum, inner thighs) and has difficulty starting urination. On examination, she has reduced anal sphincter tone, absent ankle jerks bilaterally, and reduced power in plantarflexion (L5/S1). She can walk but has a broad-based gait. Her bladder scan shows 600 mL residual volume post-void.

**Dissect the Vignette:**
- **Worsening low back pain + mechanical trigger** → raises suspicion for disc herniation as cause
- **Saddle anaesthesia** → pathognomonic for cauda equina involvement (S2–S4 dermatomes)
- **Difficulty starting urination** → indicates early bladder dysfunction (detrusor areflexia from sacral root compression)
- **Reduced anal tone** → loss of S2–S4 motor innervation to external anal sphincter
- **Absent ankle jerks** → S1 root disruption
- **Reduced plantarflexion** → S1 myotome weakness
- **Broad-based gait** → proprioceptive loss and mild weakness
- **High post-void residual** (>100 mL) → objective evidence of neurogenic bladder

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**
- **Silent CES:** Patient presents with acute urinary retention and no back pain or saddle numbness. Always check perineal sensation and rectal tone in any unexplained urinary retention in a young adult.
- **Unilateral symptoms:** May mimic a single radiculopathy (e.g., L5 radiculopathy). However, presence of bilateral symptoms or sphincter disturbance should raise alarm. Unilateral saddle numbness can occur early.
- **Normal anal tone initially:** Early CES may have only subjective perineal numbness with objectively normal tone. Don't be falsely reassured — ask about sensation and check post-void residual.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
Cauda Equina Syndrome is a clinical syndrome resulting from compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots (cauda equina) within the spinal canal below the level of the conus medullaris (around L1–L2). It is characterised by a constellation of lower motor neuron signs and autonomic dysfunction affecting bladder, bowel, and sexual function.

This topic matters because CES is a time-critical surgical emergency. Permanent neurological deficits — including paraplegia, permanent bladder/bowel incontinence, and sexual dysfunction — can occur within 24–48 hours if compression is not relieved. Missed or delayed diagnosis is one of the commonest causes of medicolegal claims in orthopaedics and neurosurgery. The pitfall is ignoring 'red flag' symptoms in patients with back pain, especially those without classic motor weakness.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The \"Why\" Behind:
The cauda equina ("horse's tail") comprises lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal nerve roots floating in CSF within the thecal sac. These roots are vulnerable to compression by any space-occupying lesion (most commonly a central disc herniation) at L4–L5 or L5–S1 levels.

**Step-by-step story:**
- **Disc herniation** → nucleus pulposus extrudes posteriorly, compressing multiple nerve roots in the central canal.
- **Venous congestion and oedema** develop distal to the compression, impairing axonal transport and causing ischaemia.
- **Nerve roots** have poor regenerative capacity; prolonged compression leads to irreversible demyelination and axonal loss.

**Why each sign occurs:**
- **Saddle anaesthesia:** Compression of sacral sensory roots (S2–S4) → loss of sensation in the perineal 'saddle' area.
- **Bladder dysfunction:** Sacral parasympathetic outflow (S2–S4) controls detrusor contraction. Lesion → detrusor areflexia → retention with overflow incontinence.
- **Bowel dysfunction:** S2–S4 also innervate the external anal sphincter (voluntary) and contribute to internal sphincter tone. Loss → faecal incontinence or constipation.
- **Lower motor neuron signs** (reduced tone, absent reflexes, flaccid weakness) at L4–S1 myotomes.
- **Sciatica often precedes CES** because a disc herniation first impinges on traversing nerve roots (e.g., S1) before compressing the whole cauda equina.

**Connect to pattern cues:** The classic vignette shows a central disc rupture — the hallmark is bilateral symptoms and sphincter involvement, distinguishing it from unilateral radiculopathy.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins):
| Cause Category | Examples | Typical Stem Clue |
|----------------|----------|-------------------|
| **Most common:** Disc herniation | Central or large paracentral L4–L5 or L5–S1 disc | Acute onset after lifting/straining; sciatica precedes saddle numbness |
| **Traumatic** | Fracture-dislocation of lumbar spine, penetrating injury, gunshot | High-energy mechanism or fall; visible deformity or neuro shock |
| **Neoplastic** | Metastases (breast, lung, prostate), primary spinal tumours (meningioma, schwannoma) | Insidious onset, night pain, weight loss, age >50, known cancer |
| **Infective** | Spinal epidural abscess, discitis, tuberculosis | Fever, night sweats, elevated CRP/ESR, IVDU, immunocompromise |
| **Inflammatory** | Guillain-Barré syndrome (rarely CES mimic), transverse myelitis | Ascending weakness, areflexia, CSF protein elevation |
| **Iatrogenic** | Post-spinal surgery haematoma, spinal anaesthesia complication | Onset within hours of procedure; anticoagulation |
| **Vascular** | Arteriovenous malformation (conus or cauda) | Stepwise or fluctuating symptoms; may worsen with exercise |
| **Congenital** | Tethered cord syndrome (presents in adults) | History of foot deformity, cutaneous stigmata (sacral dimple, hair tuft), onset during growth spurt |

**Highlight:** Central disc herniation accounts for >80% of cases. Always ask about recent heavy lifting, Valsalva-provoking activities.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic Criteria:** No single scoring system; diagnosis is clinical with MRI confirmation. The "Red Flag" criteria from the Royal College of Surgeons / NICE include:
- New-onset urinary retention or incontinence
- Bilateral or progressive lower limb weakness
- Saddle anaesthesia
- Loss of anal sphincter tone

**Key tests:**
- **Clinical examination** (see vignette): most sensitive initial tool. Check: perineal sensation (light touch and pinprick), anal tone, bulbocavernosus reflex (S2–S4), post-void bladder scan.
- **MRI whole spine (T1 and T2 sequences) — gold standard:** Shows compressive lesion (disc, abscess, tumour, haematoma) and its level. Must be done urgently (within hours). Contraindication to MRI (pacemaker etc.) → CT myelogram as alternative.
- **Bladder scan / post-void residual:** >100 mL indicates neurogenic bladder. However, normal residual does not rule out CES if symptoms are early.
- **Lumbar spine X-ray:** Limited role; may show fracture or spondylolisthesis but not disc. Do not delay MRI for X-ray.
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, ESR (infective cause), clotting (if anticoagulated), tumour markers if neoplastic suspected.

**Pattern recognition:**
- **Single finding that CONFIRMS CES:** MRI showing compression of cauda equina nerve roots with clinical correlation.
- **Single finding that RULES OUT:** Normal MRI of the lumbar spine with no compression of thecal sac — but ensure whole spine imaged if clinical suspicion high (conus lesion at T12–L1 can mimic).

**Common pitfall:** Relying on plain CT alone — CT is insensitive for disc herniation and nerve root compression. Always get MRI if clinical suspicion.
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Stabilise:** ABC, analgesia (paracetamol ± NSAIDs, but avoid high-dose opioids that may mask examination).
- **Catheterise:** If urinary retention, insert a urethral catheter. This protects the bladder from overdistension injury and allows monitoring urine output. Do NOT perform a trial without catheter.
- **Call senior:** Urgent referral to neurosurgery or orthopaedic spine team. Arrange emergency MRI.
- **Steroids:** Administration of high-dose dexamethasone (e.g., 10 mg IV stat, then 4 mg IV QDS) is controversial but often given to reduce oedema while awaiting surgery. Check local protocol.
- **Antibiotics:** If infective cause suspected (fever, raised inflammatory markers), start broad-spectrum antibiotics after blood cultures (e.g., flucloxacillin 2 g IV QDS + gentamicin empirical; adjust per microbiology).

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Definitive surgery:** Decompressive laminectomy and discectomy (or abscess drainage) is the mainstay. Ideally within 24–48 hours of symptom onset. Earlier decompression correlates with better neurological recovery.
- **Post-operative monitoring:** Continue catheter until bladder function returns (may need urodynamics). Monitor neurological status hourly initially; worsening may indicate haematoma.
- **Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) once bleeding risk deemed low (usually 24 h post-op). TED stockings.
- **Pain management:** Multimodal (paracetamol, NSAIDs, ± gabapentin for neuropathic pain). Avoid prolonged opioids.

**Long-term:**
- **Bladder rehabilitation:** Intermittent self-catheterisation (ISC) if incomplete emptying persists. Urology referral for neurogenic bladder management (anticholinergics, botulinum toxin).
- **Bowel regimen:** Laxatives + suppositories if faecal incontinence; pelvic floor physiotherapy.
- **Physiotherapy and rehabilitation:** Gait retraining, core strengthening, balance.
- **Return to work:** Advise on safe lifting, ergonomics. May require occupational health assessment.
- **Driving:** Avoid while on opioids or with motor weakness. DVLA rules: must notify if condition affects ability to drive safely.

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not perform lumbar puncture** — risk of coning in presence of compressive lesion; also, LP is for meningitis, not CES.
- **Do not give thrombolysis** — CES is not a vascular urgency; thrombolysis would be catastrophic if disc herniation.
- **Do not wait for urology opinion** before catheterising; catheter is safe and necessary.
- **Do not discharge without documented perineal sensation and bladder scan** — even if back pain improves.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | Cauda Equina Syndrome | Conus Medullaris Syndrome | Lumbar Disc Prolapse (unilateral) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS) |
|---------|-----------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Bilateral sciatica, back pain | Minimal back pain; early sphincter symptoms | Unilateral radicular leg pain, worse with sitting/Valsalva | Low back pain uncommon; paraesthesias in limbs |
| **Distribution** | Bilateral, often asymmetric leg symptoms (multilevel) | Bilateral, symmetric leg symptoms | Single dermatome/myotome | Ascending from feet, symmetric |
| **Sphincters** | Early bladder/bowel involvement (retention > incontinence) | Very early, often first sign | Usually spared | Spared unless severe autonomic involvement |
| **Reflexes** | Reduced ankle jerk (S1); knee jerk may be normal | Absent ankle jerk; reduced knee jerk if L4 involved | Reduced ankle jerk (S1) or knee jerk (L4) | Global areflexia early |
| **Sensation** | Saddle anaesthesia + non-dermatomal leg numbness | "Sacral sparing" sometimes; perineal numbness | Dermatomal sensory loss | Stocking-glove sensory loss |
| **Motor** | LMN pattern: flaccid weakness in multiple myotomes | Flaccid weakness but can be upper motor neuron if conus + cord | Single myotome weakness (e.g., foot drop) | Ascending flaccid paralysis, can involve respiratory |
| **MRI** | Compressive lesion below L1 | Focal lesion at conus (T12–L1) | Single-sided disc herniation | Normal spine; nerve root enhancement (rare) |
| **Key differentiator** | MRI shows compression of cauda equina roots | Sphincter symptoms out of proportion to leg weakness; MRI lesion at conus | Unilateral symptoms, no sphincter involvement | Areflexia, ascending pattern, CSF albuminocytologic dissociation |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
- **Permanent bladder dysfunction:** If untreated >48 hours, detrusor areflexia becomes irreversible → chronic retention, overflow incontinence, recurrent UTIs, renal impairment. Presents as inability to void, high post-void residual, 'benign' history of infrequent urination. **What to do:** Teach ISC; refer for urodynamics; lifelong monitoring.
- **Permanent bowel dysfunction:** Loss of voluntary sphincter control leads to faecal incontinence or severe constipation with impaction. Socially devastating. **What to do:** Bowel management program; biofeedback; sacral nerve stimulation (if residual function).
- **Paraplegia:** Bilateral leg weakness progressing to flaccid paralysis. If delayed beyond 48 hours, recovery of motor function is poor. **What to do:** Urgent decompression even after 48h may still salvage some.
- **Chronic pain and neuropathic pain:** Due to nerve root damage; presents as burning, stabbing pain in the perineum or legs. **What to do:** Gabapentin (300 mg PO TDS, titrate to 600 mg TDS) or pregabalin (75 mg PO BD); pain team input.
- **Autonomic dysreflexia (rare):** If a high spinal lesion co-exists; very high blood pressure in response to bladder/bowel stimuli. **What to do:** Identify trigger, sit patient up, treat hypertension with short-acting agents (e.g., nifedipine 5 mg sublingual, not SL!).

**Late-presentation pattern:** Patient arrives 1 week after onset with a urinary catheter already in place (placed by primary care for retention). They have no leg pain because they are on high-dose opioids. They now cannot walk. The key sign is persistent saddle anaesthesia — this is permanent.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonic: CES CaUDA**

- **C** – Catheter (for retention)
- **E** – Emergency MRI
- **S** – Surgery (decompressive laminectomy)

**Another: SADDLE**

- **S** – Saddle anaesthesia
- **A** – Anal sphincter laxity
- **D** – Disturbed bladder/bowel function
- **D** – Distal lower limb weakness (bilateral)
- **L** – Loss of reflexes (ankle jerk)
- **E** – Evaluate immediately

**High-yield pearls:**
1. **‘Red flag’ for back pain is any new bladder symptom** – ask about urinary stream, hesitancy, incontinence.
2. **Normal anal tone does not exclude CES** – ask specifically about perineal sensation. The patient may have only subjective numbness.
3. **The bulbocavernosus reflex** (S2–S4) – test by squeezing glans/clitoris → anal sphincter contraction. Absent reflex indicates sacral root involvement.
4. **Post-void residual >100 mL is abnormal** – do not rely on patient's report of passing urine; scan them.
5. **Bilateral leg symptoms are more concerning than unilateral** – even if mild, they suggest central compression.
6. **Time is neurological tissue** – goal is MRI within 2 hours of presentation and surgery within 24 hours.
7. **Never perform a trial without catheter in a patient with CES** – the bladder will not empty and will be overdistended, worsening injury.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Mistaking ‘saddle anaesthesia’ for ‘numbness in legs’ — saddle area is perineum, buttocks, inner thighs.
- Ordering lumbar spine X-ray and waiting for result before MRI — wastes time.
- Documenting ‘normal neurological exam’ after only checking leg power — you must check perineum and tone.
- Using opioid analgesia before full examination — masks sensory/motor findings.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant) immediately:**
- Suspected CES based on clinical findings (saddle anaesthesia, acute urinary retention, loss of anal tone, bilateral leg weakness).
- New onset of any of the above in a patient with back pain.
- Patient with known CES awaiting MRI and develops worsening weakness or respiratory compromise (very rare but can occur with high lesions).

**When to escalate to Intensive Care / Crash Team:**
- NEWS2 ≥ 5 with any sign of spinal shock (hypotension, bradycardia from spinal cord injury above T6 — neurogenic shock) — rare in CES, but if lesion is high (conus) or traumatic.
- Severe sepsis from epidural abscess with haemodynamic instability.
- Post-operative complications: spinal haematoma (new weakness, severe pain), CSF leak (postural headache, photophobia), or meningitis (fever, neck stiffness).

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2:** Escalate if ≥ 5 or if any single parameter is red (e.g., SpO2 < 91%, SBP < 90).
- **Lactate:** >2 mmol/L with signs of sepsis (if infective cause) — start sepsis 6 pathway.
- **GCS:** Any drop from baseline (e.g., < 15) — possible cerebral cause or sepsis.
- **Bladder residual:** >500 mL on initial scan is a surgical emergency and should prompt immediate senior review.

**Key point:** Do **not** wait for senior approval to take the patient for an MRI if you are concerned — arrange the scan and escalate simultaneously. Time lost is neurological outcome lost.

## Cauda Equina Syndrome

**One-liner:** Cauda Equina Syndrome (CES) is a surgical emergency caused by compression of lumbosacral nerve roots below the conus medullaris, threatening permanent paralysis and sphincter loss.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic Vignette:** A 45-year-old woman presents to ED with a 2-day history of worsening lower back pain after lifting a heavy box. She now reports numbness in the 'saddle area' (buttocks, perineum, inner thighs) and has difficulty starting urination. On examination, she has reduced anal sphincter tone, absent ankle jerks bilaterally, and reduced power in plantarflexion (L5/S1). She can walk but has a broad-based gait. Her bladder scan shows 600 mL residual volume post-void.

**Dissect the Vignette:**
- **Worsening low back pain + mechanical trigger** → raises suspicion for disc herniation as cause
- **Saddle anaesthesia** → pathognomonic for cauda equina involvement (S2–S4 dermatomes)
- **Difficulty starting urination** → indicates early bladder dysfunction (detrusor areflexia from sacral root compression)
- **Reduced anal tone** → loss of S2–S4 motor innervation to external anal sphincter
- **Absent ankle jerks** → S1 root disruption
- **Reduced plantarflexion** → S1 myotome weakness
- **Broad-based gait** → proprioceptive loss and mild weakness
- **High post-void residual** (>100 mL) → objective evidence of neurogenic bladder

**Atypical Presentations That May Trick You:**
- **Silent CES:** Patient presents with acute urinary retention and no back pain or saddle numbness. Always check perineal sensation and rectal tone in any unexplained urinary retention in a young adult.
- **Unilateral symptoms:** May mimic a single radiculopathy (e.g., L5 radiculopathy). However, presence of bilateral symptoms or sphincter disturbance should raise alarm. Unilateral saddle numbness can occur early.
- **Normal anal tone initially:** Early CES may have only subjective perineal numbness with objectively normal tone. Don't be falsely reassured — ask about sensation and check post-void residual.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

Cauda Equina Syndrome is a clinical syndrome resulting from compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots (cauda equina) within the spinal canal below the level of the conus medullaris (around L1–L2). It is characterised by a constellation of lower motor neuron signs and autonomic dysfunction affecting bladder, bowel, and sexual function.

This topic matters because CES is a time-critical surgical emergency. Permanent neurological deficits — including paraplegia, permanent bladder/bowel incontinence, and sexual dysfunction — can occur within 24–48 hours if compression is not relieved. Missed or delayed diagnosis is one of the commonest causes of medicolegal claims in orthopaedics and neurosurgery. The pitfall is ignoring 'red flag' symptoms in patients with back pain, especially those without classic motor weakness.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The "Why" Behind The Findings

The cauda equina ("horse's tail") comprises lumbar, sacral, and coccygeal nerve roots floating in CSF within the thecal sac. These roots are vulnerable to compression by any space-occupying lesion (most commonly a central disc herniation) at L4–L5 or L5–S1 levels.

**Step-by-step story:**
- **Disc herniation** → nucleus pulposus extrudes posteriorly, compressing multiple nerve roots in the central canal.
- **Venous congestion and oedema** develop distal to the compression, impairing axonal transport and causing ischaemia.
- **Nerve roots** have poor regenerative capacity; prolonged compression leads to irreversible demyelination and axonal loss.

**Why each sign occurs:**
- **Saddle anaesthesia:** Compression of sacral sensory roots (S2–S4) → loss of sensation in the perineal 'saddle' area.
- **Bladder dysfunction:** Sacral parasympathetic outflow (S2–S4) controls detrusor contraction. Lesion → detrusor areflexia → retention with overflow incontinence.
- **Bowel dysfunction:** S2–S4 also innervate the external anal sphincter (voluntary) and contribute to internal sphincter tone. Loss → faecal incontinence or constipation.
- **Lower motor neuron signs** (reduced tone, absent reflexes, flaccid weakness) at L4–S1 myotomes.
- **Sciatica often precedes CES** because a disc herniation first impinges on traversing nerve roots (e.g., S1) before compressing the whole cauda equina.

**Connect to pattern cues:** The classic vignette shows a central disc rupture — the hallmark is bilateral symptoms and sphincter involvement, distinguishing it from unilateral radiculopathy.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (Organised in Bins)

| Cause Category | Examples | Typical Stem Clue |
|----------------|----------|-------------------|
| **Most common:** Disc herniation | Central or large paracentral L4–L5 or L5–S1 disc | Acute onset after lifting/straining; sciatica precedes saddle numbness |
| **Traumatic** | Fracture-dislocation of lumbar spine, penetrating injury, gunshot | High-energy mechanism or fall; visible deformity or neuro shock |
| **Neoplastic** | Metastases (breast, lung, prostate), primary spinal tumours (meningioma, schwannoma) | Insidious onset, night pain, weight loss, age >50, known cancer |
| **Infective** | Spinal epidural abscess, discitis, tuberculosis | Fever, night sweats, elevated CRP/ESR, IVDU, immunocompromise |
| **Inflammatory** | Guillain-Barré syndrome (rarely CES mimic), transverse myelitis | Ascending weakness, areflexia, CSF protein elevation |
| **Iatrogenic** | Post-spinal surgery haematoma, spinal anaesthesia complication | Onset within hours of procedure; anticoagulation |
| **Vascular** | Arteriovenous malformation (conus or cauda) | Stepwise or fluctuating symptoms; may worsen with exercise |
| **Congenital** | Tethered cord syndrome (presents in adults) | History of foot deformity, cutaneous stigmata (sacral dimple, hair tuft), onset during growth spurt |

**Highlight:** Central disc herniation accounts for >80% of cases. Always ask about recent heavy lifting, Valsalva-provoking activities.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic Criteria:** No single scoring system; diagnosis is clinical with MRI confirmation. The "Red Flag" criteria from the Royal College of Surgeons / NICE include:
- New-onset urinary retention or incontinence
- Bilateral or progressive lower limb weakness
- Saddle anaesthesia
- Loss of anal sphincter tone

**Key tests:**
- **Clinical examination** (see vignette): most sensitive initial tool. Check: perineal sensation (light touch and pinprick), anal tone, bulbocavernosus reflex (S2–S4), post-void bladder scan.
- **MRI whole spine (T1 and T2 sequences) — gold standard:** Shows compressive lesion (disc, abscess, tumour, haematoma) and its level. Must be done urgently (within hours). Contraindication to MRI (pacemaker etc.) → CT myelogram as alternative.
- **Bladder scan / post-void residual:** >100 mL indicates neurogenic bladder. However, normal residual does not rule out CES if symptoms are early.
- **Lumbar spine X-ray:** Limited role; may show fracture or spondylolisthesis but not disc. Do not delay MRI for X-ray.
- **Bloods:** FBC, CRP, ESR (infective cause), clotting (if anticoagulated), tumour markers if neoplastic suspected.

- **Single finding that RULES OUT:** Normal MRI of the lumbar spine with no compression of thecal sac — but ensure whole spine imaged if clinical suspicion high (conus lesion at T12–L1 can mimic).

**Common pitfall:** Relying on plain CT alone — CT is insensitive for disc herniation and nerve root compression. Always get MRI if clinical suspicion.

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour):**
- **Stabilise:** ABC, analgesia (paracetamol ± NSAIDs, but avoid high-dose opioids that may mask examination).
- **Catheterise:** If urinary retention, insert a urethral catheter. This protects the bladder from overdistension injury and allows monitoring urine output. Do NOT perform a trial without catheter.
- **Call senior:** Urgent referral to neurosurgery or orthopaedic spine team. Arrange emergency MRI.
- **Steroids:** Administration of high-dose dexamethasone (e.g., 10 mg IV stat, then 4 mg IV QDS) is controversial but often given to reduce oedema while awaiting surgery. Check local protocol.
- **Antibiotics:** If infective cause suspected (fever, raised inflammatory markers), start broad-spectrum antibiotics after blood cultures (e.g., flucloxacillin 2 g IV QDS + gentamicin empirical; adjust per microbiology).

**Ward (24–72 hours):**
- **Definitive surgery:** Decompressive laminectomy and discectomy (or abscess drainage) is the mainstay. Ideally within 24–48 hours of symptom onset. Earlier decompression correlates with better neurological recovery.
- **Post-operative monitoring:** Continue catheter until bladder function returns (may need urodynamics). Monitor neurological status hourly initially; worsening may indicate haematoma.
- **Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis:** LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC OD) once bleeding risk deemed low (usually 24 h post-op). TED stockings.
- **Pain management:** Multimodal (paracetamol, NSAIDs, ± gabapentin for neuropathic pain). Avoid prolonged opioids.

**Long-term:**
- **Bladder rehabilitation:** Intermittent self-catheterisation (ISC) if incomplete emptying persists. Urology referral for neurogenic bladder management (anticholinergics, botulinum toxin).
- **Bowel regimen:** Laxatives + suppositories if faecal incontinence; pelvic floor physiotherapy.
- **Physiotherapy and rehabilitation:** Gait retraining, core strengthening, balance.
- **Return to work:** Advise on safe lifting, ergonomics. May require occupational health assessment.
- **Driving:** Avoid while on opioids or with motor weakness. DVLA rules: must notify if condition affects ability to drive safely.

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not perform lumbar puncture** — risk of coning in presence of compressive lesion; also, LP is for meningitis, not CES.
- **Do not give thrombolysis** — CES is not a vascular urgency; thrombolysis would be catastrophic if disc herniation.
- **Do not wait for urology opinion** before catheterising; catheter is safe and necessary.
- **Do not discharge without documented perineal sensation and bladder scan** — even if back pain improves.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | Cauda Equina Syndrome | Conus Medullaris Syndrome | Lumbar Disc Prolapse (unilateral) | Guillain-Barré Syndrome (GBS) |
|---------|-----------------------|---------------------------|----------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Pain** | Bilateral sciatica, back pain | Minimal back pain; early sphincter symptoms | Unilateral radicular leg pain, worse with sitting/Valsalva | Low back pain uncommon; paraesthesias in limbs |
| **Distribution** | Bilateral, often asymmetric leg symptoms (multilevel) | Bilateral, symmetric leg symptoms | Single dermatome/myotome | Ascending from feet, symmetric |
| **Sphincters** | Early bladder/bowel involvement (retention > incontinence) | Very early, often first sign | Usually spared | Spared unless severe autonomic involvement |
| **Reflexes** | Reduced ankle jerk (S1); knee jerk may be normal | Absent ankle jerk; reduced knee jerk if L4 involved | Reduced ankle jerk (S1) or knee jerk (L4) | Global areflexia early |
| **Sensation** | Saddle anaesthesia + non-dermatomal leg numbness | "Sacral sparing" sometimes; perineal numbness | Dermatomal sensory loss | Stocking-glove sensory loss |
| **Motor** | LMN pattern: flaccid weakness in multiple myotomes | Flaccid weakness but can be upper motor neuron if conus + cord | Single myotome weakness (e.g., foot drop) | Ascending flaccid paralysis, can involve respiratory |
| **MRI** | Compressive lesion below L1 | Focal lesion at conus (T12–L1) | Single-sided disc herniation | Normal spine; nerve root enhancement (rare) |
| **Key differentiator** | MRI shows compression of cauda equina roots | Sphincter symptoms out of proportion to leg weakness; MRI lesion at conus | Unilateral symptoms, no sphincter involvement | Areflexia, ascending pattern, CSF albuminocytologic dissociation |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

- **Permanent bladder dysfunction:** If untreated >48 hours, detrusor areflexia becomes irreversible → chronic retention, overflow incontinence, recurrent UTIs, renal impairment. Presents as inability to void, high post-void residual, 'benign' history of infrequent urination. **What to do:** Teach ISC; refer for urodynamics; lifelong monitoring.
- **Permanent bowel dysfunction:** Loss of voluntary sphincter control leads to faecal incontinence or severe constipation with impaction. Socially devastating. **What to do:** Bowel management program; biofeedback; sacral nerve stimulation (if residual function).
- **Paraplegia:** Bilateral leg weakness progressing to flaccid paralysis. If delayed beyond 48 hours, recovery of motor function is poor. **What to do:** Urgent decompression even after 48h may still salvage some.
- **Chronic pain and neuropathic pain:** Due to nerve root damage; presents as burning, stabbing pain in the perineum or legs. **What to do:** Gabapentin (300 mg PO TDS, titrate to 600 mg TDS) or pregabalin (75 mg PO BD); pain team input.
- **Autonomic dysreflexia (rare):** If a high spinal lesion co-exists; very high blood pressure in response to bladder/bowel stimuli. **What to do:** Identify trigger, sit patient up, treat hypertension with short-acting agents (e.g., nifedipine 5 mg sublingual, not SL!).

**Late-presentation pattern:** Patient arrives 1 week after onset with a urinary catheter already in place (placed by primary care for retention). They have no leg pain because they are on high-dose opioids. They now cannot walk. The key sign is persistent saddle anaesthesia — this is permanent.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonic: CES CaUDA**

- **C** – Catheter (for retention)
- **E** – Emergency MRI
- **S** – Surgery (decompressive laminectomy)

**Another: SADDLE**

- **S** – Saddle anaesthesia
- **A** – Anal sphincter laxity
- **D** – Disturbed bladder/bowel function
- **D** – Distal lower limb weakness (bilateral)
- **L** – Loss of reflexes (ankle jerk)
- **E** – Evaluate immediately

**High-yield pearls:**
1. **‘Red flag’ for back pain is any new bladder symptom** – ask about urinary stream, hesitancy, incontinence.
2. **Normal anal tone does not exclude CES** – ask specifically about perineal sensation. The patient may have only subjective numbness.
3. **The bulbocavernosus reflex** (S2–S4) – test by squeezing glans/clitoris → anal sphincter contraction. Absent reflex indicates sacral root involvement.
4. **Post-void residual >100 mL is abnormal** – do not rely on patient's report of passing urine; scan them.
5. **Bilateral leg symptoms are more concerning than unilateral** – even if mild, they suggest central compression.
6. **Time is neurological tissue** – goal is MRI within 2 hours of presentation and surgery within 24 hours.
7. **Never perform a trial without catheter in a patient with CES** – the bladder will not empty and will be overdistended, worsening injury.

**Common exam/ward mistakes:**
- Mistaking ‘saddle anaesthesia’ for ‘numbness in legs’ — saddle area is perineum, buttocks, inner thighs.
- Ordering lumbar spine X-ray and waiting for result before MRI — wastes time.
- Documenting ‘normal neurological exam’ after only checking leg power — you must check perineum and tone.
- Using opioid analgesia before full examination — masks sensory/motor findings.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call your senior (registrar/consultant) immediately:**
- Suspected CES based on clinical findings (saddle anaesthesia, acute urinary retention, loss of anal tone, bilateral leg weakness).
- New onset of any of the above in a patient with back pain.
- Patient with known CES awaiting MRI and develops worsening weakness or respiratory compromise (very rare but can occur with high lesions).

**When to escalate to Intensive Care / Crash Team:**
- NEWS2 ≥ 5 with any sign of spinal shock (hypotension, bradycardia from spinal cord injury above T6 — neurogenic shock) — rare in CES, but if lesion is high (conus) or traumatic.
- Severe sepsis from epidural abscess with haemodynamic instability.
- Post-operative complications: spinal haematoma (new weakness, severe pain), CSF leak (postural headache, photophobia), or meningitis (fever, neck stiffness).

**Specific thresholds:**
- **NEWS2:** Escalate if ≥ 5 or if any single parameter is red (e.g., SpO2 < 91%, SBP < 90).
- **Lactate:** >2 mmol/L with signs of sepsis (if infective cause) — start sepsis 6 pathway.
- **GCS:** Any drop from baseline (e.g., < 15) — possible cerebral cause or sepsis.
- **Bladder residual:** >500 mL on initial scan is a surgical emergency and should prompt immediate senior review.

**Key point:** Do **not** wait for senior approval to take the patient for an MRI if you are concerned — arrange the scan and escalate simultaneously. Time lost is neurological outcome lost.
